chunk_summary: Admission criteria for infants under 6 months with SAM focus on feeding effectiveness, physiologic stability, and specific danger signs. Anthropometric measures like weight-for-length <-3 SD are used when feasible, with inpatient care prioritized for severe cases.
learning_objectives: Understand the admission criteria for infants under 6 months with severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Identify danger signs and clinical triggers for inpatient care in malnourished infants.
Differentiate between inpatient and outpatient care pathways for infants with SAM.
Learn the role of feeding effectiveness and caregiver capacity in disposition decisions.
key_facts: Infants under 6 months with SAM should not be assessed using standard MUAC thresholds or RUTF appetite tests.
Admission is required if danger signs like lethargy, convulsions, hypothermia, or respiratory distress are present.
Anthropometric criteria include weight-for-length <-3 SD when length >45 cm is reliably measured.
Outpatient care is only appropriate if feeding is effective, there is no edema, and weekly follow-up is possible.
Initial inpatient priorities include preventing fasting, maintaining warmth, correcting hypoglycemia, and treating infections.
reference_quote: Admit an infant <6 months with acute malnutrition if any danger sign is present (lethargy, convulsions, hypothermia, hypoglycemia, respiratory distress, persistent vomiting).
document_key_topics: Admission criteria for infants under 6 months with SAM
